title: introduce-guided-rag-atelier-quickstart-with-composable-adapters
preview:
Motivation: Provide a compact, reproducible developer quickstart that helps engineers iterate on retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) flows with composable tool adapters (HTTP, SQL, FS) and snapshot-driven end-to-end assertions. This quickstart targets local-first authoring (devcontainer + headless indexer) and a small wasm-based reranker so teams can prototype without cloud dependencies.

What this PR adds: 1) examples/guided-atelier/ — a self-contained quickstart with stepwise authoring UI (lightweight React), a toy indexer (local sqlite + embedding shim), and three adapter examples (http_adapter.ts, sql_adapter.ts, fs_adapter.ts). 2) devcontainer/ and .devcontainer.json to run the quickstart with one-click in VS Code; the container spins up a headless indexer and a local wasm reranker. 3) docs/quickstarts/guided-atelier.md — a new quickstart page for the docs site with step-by-step runbook and troubleshooting. 4) test/snapshots/guided-atelier/*.snap — snapshot assertions for the main scenarios to catch regressions in generated tool calls and structured outputs. 5) small updates to examples/README index to link the new quickstart and to the SDK examples to demonstrate tool-calling with structured output schemas.

Checklist: 
- [x] Add quickstart code and documentation
- [x] Add devcontainer + local infra scripts
- [x] Add snapshot-based e2e smoke tests (CI)
- [x] Add example adapter implementations (HTTP/SQL/FS) and adapter README
- [x] Add minimal LICENSE-friendly test data and seed index (under examples/guided-atelier/data)

How to run locally: 
1) Open repo in VS Code and select the Guided Atelier devcontainer (or run ./examples/guided-atelier/bootstrap.sh locally). 
2) The container starts an indexer on port 9100 and a tiny wasm reranker on 9111. 
3) Run yarn start in examples/guided-atelier to open the authoring UI at http://localhost:3000. 
4) Use the built-in scenario runner to record a session; rerun the snapshot tests with yarn test:snap.

Commit summary (high-level):  - Add guided-atelier quickstart skeleton and core services. - Implement three adapter examples and adapter API. - Add devcontainer and bootstrap scripts. - Add docs quickstart page and TOC link. - Add snapshot tests and CI job.

Files (high-level changes): examples/guided-atelier/README.md, examples/guided-atelier/src/app.tsx, examples/guided-atelier/src/adapters/http_adapter.ts, examples/guided-atelier/src/adapters/sql_adapter.ts, examples/guided-atelier/src/adapters/fs_adapter.ts, examples/guided-atelier/devcontainer/Dockerfile, examples/guided-atelier/devcontainer/devcontainer.json, examples/guided-atelier/bootstrap.sh, examples/guided-atelier/data/seed_index.json, docs/quickstarts/guided-atelier.md, test/snapshots/guided-atelier/authoring-session.snap, .github/workflows/guided-atelier-ci.yml, examples/README.md (toc addition).

Testing and CI: Added a dedicated CI workflow .github/workflows/guided-atelier-ci.yml that boots a small node service and runs snapshot smoke tests. The first run showed a flaky e2e step (wasm-reranker timeout) which was fixed by increasing startup waits and adding a retry wrapper. CI matrix includes node 18/20 and runs on ubuntu-22.04.

Backward compatibility and migration notes: The adapters implement a tiny adapter contract that mirrors our examples SDK but does not change public SDK types. No breaking changes expected. Snapshot tests are opt-in; teams can run with UPDATE_SNAP=1 to refresh.

Related linear tickets: ENG-7891 (quickstart), ENG-7932 (devcontainer infra), PM-324 (docs quickstart page). Related discussion: design thread in Linear ENG-7891 and a short RFC in #dev-examples channel.

Reason this is unique: Unlike previous large RAG blueprints, Guided Atelier focuses on 'local authoring fidelity' — deterministic snapshots, small wasm reranker to approximate production re-rank behavior, and modular adapters to teach patterns for tool-calling and structured outputs in a contained environment.

Notes for reviewers: Most of the logic lives in examples/guided-atelier/src and is intentionally small. Focus review on adapter contract clarity, devcontainer reproducibility, and snapshot assertions. The docs page includes a quick troubleshooting section for common platform issues (ports, container memory).

Merge strategy: squash-and-merge with a single summarized commit message.

Release notes: Add new quickstart: Guided RAG Atelier (local-first authoring + composable adapters + snapshot tests).

title: Introduce compact RAG Playwright integration and local scenario recorder
preview: Motivation: Developers frequently ask for a small, reproducible harness that demonstrates RAG workflows end-to-end in a browser context while capturing deterministic telemetry for local debugging and CI. This PR introduces a lightweight Playwright integration plus a local scenario recorder that can capture step-by-step interactions, network stubs, and RAG tool calls for later replay or CI assertions. --- What this changes: added a new example app (examples/rag-playwright) that runs a tiny React UI and a Node-based recorder; a Playwright test suite that exercises common RAG scenarios (search->tool-call->compose->verify); devcontainer + npm scripts to run recorder and replay flows; documentation and a short Quickstart on how to record scenarios, attach telemetry to Redwood Console, and produce reproducible regression tests. --- Checklist: [x] Add example app and recorder runtime; [x] Add Playwright test harness and sample scenarios; [x] Document developer Quickstart and recorder API; [x] Hook basic telemetry events to the recorder for token/latency traces; [x] CI smoketest that replays recorded scenarios. --- Links: demo GIF + usage guide in README, local playback CLI docs at /docs/local-playback, related Linear ENG-4129. This description includes the end-to-end motivation, usage notes, and where to find artifacts in the tree.

title: Introduce stubbed tool-adapter playground and auth examples
preview: Motivation: teams frequently need a safe, repeatable way to prototype agents that call external tools (storage, calendars, webhooks, connectors) without setting up real credentials or risking side effects. This PR adds a small, documented playground that: 1) provides a plugin-style 'tool adapter' API for wiring connectors to agents; 2) includes stubbed adapters for S3-like storage, Slack webhook, and Google Drive listing; 3) demonstrates a minimal OAuth/ephemeral-credential exchange shim for local development; 4) wires structured-output contracts and example prompt templates to make tool calls observable in traces and telemetry. What this changes (high level): - new examples/plugin-toolbinding/ directory with 3 example adapters and a lightweight dev server that runs them in-process - docs/examples/toolbinding.md with usage, security considerations, and contract schemas - examples/playgrounds/tool-adapter-replay/ that contains a session recorder/replayer for deterministic dev runs - test/smoke/toolbinding-smoke.sh and a jest-based harness for the adapters - small additions to examples/shared/schema/ for structured-output validators Checklist: - [x] examples and README - [x] smoke tests + CI job - [x] docs pages + crosslinks to authoring guide - [x] explicit guidance on when to replace stubs with real connectors Local quickstart (single continuous command sequence): git checkout feature/plugin-toolbinding-playground && yarn && yarn start:playground Visit http://localhost:3030 to open the playground UI, click 'Create session' to spawn a sandboxed agent with stubbed adapters, and run the sample 'email-extract-and-store' scenario to see tool calls appear in the trace. Security notes: stubs intentionally accept any token in dev mode; docs/examples/toolbinding.md includes production migration steps (KMS, HSM, RBAC, SCIM). Linear/Related: ENG-1832 (playground), PM-412 (dev experience).

title: Introduce interactive RAG simulation shell CLI with tool stubs and session export
preview:
Motivation: teams repeatedly ask for a lightweight, replayable local playground to iterate on retrieval-augmented generation prompts, tool-calling behavior, and agent orchestration without spinning up full infra. This PR introduces `rag-sim`, an interactive CLI 'simulation shell' that lets authors run stepwise scenarios, inject fake tool responses, snapshot sessions, and export deterministic seeds for CI/eval pipelines.

What this PR does: adds a small TypeScript CLI shim and example playbooks that emulate the Redwood runtime semantics (context window simulation, KV cache warmup stub, and streaming output). The shell supports step, run, rewind, and patch commands so developers can test partial tool failures and fallback logic quickly. Sessions can be exported as JSON or a compact replay script that our e2e harness can consume. The goal is low-friction iteration for authoring and regression tests; this is intended as examples/dev tooling only (not for production serving).

Checklist:
- [x] Implement CLI core loop and command parser
- [x] Add tool stub interface and example tool responses
- [x] Add three authored playbooks demonstrating common failure modes (retrieval miss, tool timeout, malformed tool output)
- [x] Documentation quickstart under docs/quickstarts/rag-sim-shell.md with screenshots and step-by-step commands
- [x] Unit tests for CLI command parsing and replay export
- [x] CI workflow to run smoke replay scenarios on PRs

Usage (short): `npx @redwood/rag-sim playbooks/example-localsearch.yml --export session1.json` then `npx @redwood/rag-sim replay session1.json --stepwise`. The README includes more examples and a sample Linear link for the test scenarios.

Commits summary: three commits - initial CLI scaffold and parser; add tool-stubs + three example playbooks; docs, tests, and CI workflow. See changed files below.

Why this is different: instead of a full SDK change, this is a focused dev-oriented sandbox that mimics runtime decision points (cache hits, batching signals, and tool fallbacks) so prompt authors and solutions engineers can iterate without heavy infra. It also provides deterministic exports to improve flaky-eval reproducibility.

Risks and mitigation: the CLI vends no production credentials and uses only local stubs by default. We added linting, typechecks, and a small smoke CI to catch regressions.

Related: ENG-3421 (authoring ergonomics), PM-58 (playbook templates).

title: introduce-rag-microservice-tool-calling-docker-quickstart
preview: Add a compact, docker-first RAG microservice quickstart that demonstrates function/tool-calling, streaming responses, and a minimal KV cache for low-latency private deployments. Motivation: customers frequently ask for a small, deployable reference that shows how to wire Redwood SDK into a tiny service that handles document retrieval, model orchestration, and calls external tools (search + knowledge connector) with clear observability hooks. This PR delivers: 1) examples/rag-microservice/ - a ready-to-run template (Dockerfile + docker-compose) that boots a single-node instance with an embedded retriever, Redwood SDK integration, and a simple tool interface for invoking a mock search API. 2) README quickstart that walks through local Docker run, env var configuration for hosted vs. private endpoints, and sample curl/SDK calls for streaming. 3) src/agent.ts - an opinionated agent orchestration example that shows stepwise tool-calling (retrieve -> call tool -> synthesize -> stream) and includes backpressure-friendly streaming hooks. 4) docs/quickstarts/rag-microservice.md - a short doc page for the docs site (to be pulled into the quickstarts index) focused on deployment modes and production considerations (batching, KV cache warmup, model pinning). 5) Integration test and CI job to validate startup and a basic end-to-end conversation sample. Checklist: - [x] Minimal runnable example (Docker) - [x] Clear env var examples for redwood API key / private endpoint - [x] Streaming example with SDK snippets for JS and Python - [x] Observability pointers (which telemetry hooks to wire) - [x] Integration test in CI that runs container and POSTs a sample query Notes on differences vs existing RAG quickstarts: this template is intentionally compact and implements tool-calling as explicit function stubs (mock_search, metadata_indexer) so customers can replace with their production connectors easily. It emphasizes local Docker-first flow and opts for a lightweight on-disk document index rather than a full vector DB to keep the quickstart low-friction. Compatibility: works with Redwood SDK >= 0.9.0; recommends model variants that support streaming token-level responses. Backwards-incompatible changes: none. Related Linear tasks: ENG-4991 implement micro-quickstart; PM-221 doc alignment for quickstarts. Merge rationale: small, self-contained, high-value example for engineering onboarding and support triage.

title: Add compact RAG dependency-injection samples and browser dev proxy
preview:
Motivation: Teams repeatedly ask for composable RAG patterns that are easy to reason about and test. This PR adds a compact dependency-injection (DI) oriented samples folder demonstrating how to compose retrievers, indexers, and tool-calling agents in a small, testable way. It also includes a tiny browser dev-proxy to simplify local authoring (pre-signed snippet proxy + telemetry stubs).

What this PR contains: a) an authoring README with usage patterns and mental model, b) three TypeScript sample modules that show layered DI: core client wiring, test doubles for retrievers, and a toy tool-caller adapter; c) a minimal browser proxy (node/express) that proxies indexed snippet requests and fakes auth for local dev; d) e2e smoke scripts and a lightweight smoke CI step.

Design notes: the DI layer is intentionally minimal — it uses factory functions and explicit interfaces rather than a heavy DI framework to keep examples portable across SDKs. The sample demonstrates: - swapping a fake in-memory retriever for a production remote retriever - how to inject a caching policy and KV-prefixing for session state - wiring a tool-caller that exposes a single predictable API for mocking in tests.

Checklist:
- [x] Add samples and README
- [x] Add browser dev-proxy with README and startup script
- [x] Add smoke CI job (runs only on docs/example changes)
- [x] Unit tests for DI factories using fakes
- [x] Document migration guidance for teams using legacy monolithic examples

Testing and verification: Ran local smoke scripts and the CI smoke job on branch. Unit tests for the DI factories cover dependency substitution and a small deterministic retrieval scenario. Verified the browser proxy can be started with npm run dev and that it serves the sample snippet API (GET /_local/snippets).

Related: reference design in ENG-4321; this complements the rag-playbook examples by focusing on wiring and testability. Suggestions for follow-ups: add equivalent Python/Go minimal examples and a short console tutorial that links into these samples.

title: Introduce RAG micro-bundle and local plugin-runner for rapid prototyping
preview:
Motivation: enable engineers to prototype retrieval-augmented agents and tool-calling flows locally without pulling the entire examples monorepo. The micro-bundle packages minimal adapters (HTTP tool, file-tool, open-search stub), a CLI to run a dev-mode agent, and a tiny WASM worker for sandboxed plugin logic. This targets developer experience and workshop use-cases: lower friction to teach RAG patterns and iterate on tool integrations.

Summary: single compact example directory (examples/rag-microbundle) that can be run with `yarn dev` or the included Go CLI. The bundle includes: a) README with step-by-step quickstart, b) example prompts and prompt templates, c) local tool runner (Node + WASM shim) that demonstrates function/tool calling with an LLM stub, and d) a telemetry hint file showing recommended tracing hooks for quick troubleshooting.

Checklist: - [x] Minimal CLI scaffold that starts an agent and local tools - [x] WASM shim for untrusted plugin execution (example: small arithmetic tool) - [x] HTTP tool adapter and file-indexer stub - [x] Clear README with quickstart commands and expected outputs - [x] Tests: smoke script for local run and a Jest test for the plugin runner - [x] Docs crosslink to docs site quickstarts and devcontainer template

How to try locally (short): 1) git checkout feature/rag-microbundle-local-plugin-runner 2) cd examples/rag-microbundle 3) yarn install && yarn dev 4) open http://localhost:3001 to interact with the agent or run `./bin/rag-cli run --example arithmetic` for a terminal demo.

Commits summary: - feat: initial microbundle scaffold and README - feat: add local HTTP tool adapter and file indexer stub - feat: add wasm shim and TypeScript runner - test: add smoke e2e script and Jest unit for wasm runner - ci: add workflow for devbundle smoke-run on PRs - docs: link microbundle from examples index

Files changed (high level): examples/rag-microbundle/README.md, examples/rag-microbundle/package.json, examples/rag-microbundle/src/agent.ts, examples/rag-microbundle/src/tools/http-tool.ts, examples/rag-microbundle/wasm/arith_worker.ts, examples/rag-microbundle/bin/rag-cli.go, examples/rag-microbundle/.github/workflows/devbundle.yml, examples/rag-microbundle/tests/wasm-runner.test.ts, docs/examples-index.md (crosslink).

CI & validation: initial CI run failed lint (unused import in wasm runner) and a test flake on the smoke-run (timing issue). Fixed lint in commit `fix: wasm runner lint` and bumped Jest timeout in `test: increase wasm timeout`. After fixes the GitHub Actions workflow passed all checks. This PR also includes a small Dockerfile used by the smoke CI job; we intentionally keep the image minimal to avoid long pulls in CI.

Review notes & trade-offs: - Security: the WASM shim is intentionally minimal (no WASI filesystem access) and demonstrates safe sandboxing, but it is not production hardened — callout in README and docs. - Scope: kept the example intentionally small; more adapters (SQL, S3) are left as follow-ups to avoid large diffs. - UX: CLI offers a `--dev` flag to enable verbose tracing; we added a small telemetry hint file `telemetry.md` describing what to wire into Redwood Console for deeper inspection.

Related: links to ENG-2345 (prototype RAG workshop) and PM-987 (developer onboarding initiative).
text:
Motivation: enable engineers to prototype retrieval-augmented agents and tool-calling flows locally without pulling the entire examples monorepo. The micro-bundle packages minimal adapters (HTTP tool, file-tool, open-search stub), a CLI to run a dev-mode agent, and a tiny WASM worker for sandboxed plugin logic. This targets developer experience and workshop use-cases: lower friction to teach RAG patterns and iterate on tool integrations.

Summary: single compact example directory (examples/rag-microbundle) that can be run with `yarn dev` or the included Go CLI. The bundle includes: a) README with step-by-step quickstart, b) example prompts and prompt templates, c) local tool runner (Node + WASM shim) that demonstrates function/tool calling with an LLM stub, and d) a telemetry hint file showing recommended tracing hooks for quick troubleshooting.

Checklist: - [x] Minimal CLI scaffold that starts an agent and local tools - [x] WASM shim for untrusted plugin execution (example: small arithmetic tool) - [x] HTTP tool adapter and file-indexer stub - [x] Clear README with quickstart commands and expected outputs - [x] Tests: smoke script for local run and a Jest test for the plugin runner - [x] Docs crosslink to docs site quickstarts and devcontainer template

How to try locally (short): 1) git checkout feature/rag-microbundle-local-plugin-runner 2) cd examples/rag-microbundle 3) yarn install && yarn dev 4) open http://localhost:3001 to interact with the agent or run `./bin/rag-cli run --example arithmetic` for a terminal demo.

Commits summary: - feat: initial microbundle scaffold and README - feat: add local HTTP tool adapter and file indexer stub - feat: add wasm shim and TypeScript runner - test: add smoke e2e script and Jest unit for wasm runner - ci: add workflow for devbundle smoke-run on PRs - docs: link microbundle from examples index

Files changed (high level): examples/rag-microbundle/README.md, examples/rag-microbundle/package.json, examples/rag-microbundle/src/agent.ts, examples/rag-microbundle/src/tools/http-tool.ts, examples/rag-microbundle/wasm/arith_worker.ts, examples/rag-microbundle/bin/rag-cli.go, examples/rag-microbundle/.github/workflows/devbundle.yml, examples/rag-microbundle/tests/wasm-runner.test.ts, docs/examples-index.md (crosslink).

CI & validation: initial CI run failed lint (unused import in wasm runner) and a test flake on the smoke-run (timing issue). Fixed lint in commit `fix: wasm runner lint` and bumped Jest timeout in `test: increase wasm timeout`. After fixes the GitHub Actions workflow passed all checks. This PR also includes a small Dockerfile used by the smoke CI job; we intentionally keep the image minimal to avoid long pulls in CI.

Review notes & trade-offs: - Security: the WASM shim is intentionally minimal (no WASI filesystem access) and demonstrates safe sandboxing, but it is not production hardened — callout in README and docs. - Scope: kept the example intentionally small; more adapters (SQL, S3) are left as follow-ups to avoid large diffs. - UX: CLI offers a `--dev` flag to enable verbose tracing; we added a small telemetry hint file `telemetry.md` describing what to wire into Redwood Console for deeper inspection.

title: Introduce RAG plugin SDK + extensibility quickstart
preview:
Context: Customers often need to extend Redwood RAG flows with custom third-party adapters (custom vector connectors, proprietary tool wrappers, or edge-specialized preprocessors). The goal of this PR is to ship a minimal, well-documented RAG Plugin SDK and a hands-on quickstart that demonstrates how to author, test, and publish a local plugin that integrates with Redwood tool-calling and retrieval pipeline.

Motivation: Existing quickstarts show how to wire RAG flows end-to-end but don't provide a structured pattern for third-party plugin authors (type-safe lifecycle, capability discovery, and test harness). This makes it hard for partner teams and community contributors to build resilient adapters that behave correctly under streaming and fallback conditions.

What I changed: Added a new quickstart directory (examples/rag-plugin-sdk-quickstart) containing:
- A compact RAG Plugin SDK (TypeScript) that exposes a Plugin interface, capability descriptors, and a small adapter runtime for local testing. The SDK focuses on: typed request/response shapes, async streaming helper, and KV-prefetch hooks for retrieval optimization.
- Two example adapters: postgres-vector-adapter (shows paginated upserts and vector search shim) and pdf-tool-adapter (example tool that extracts, chunks, and annotates docs). Both include integration tests that run against a lightweight test double (sqlite in-memory for vectors, headless pdf fixture).
- Developer experience files: docs/quickstart.md (step-by-step), .devcontainer with docker-compose and a local redis/service mesh, a light GH Actions smoke-check workflow, and example npm scripts for build/test/watch.
- Docs site integration: added a short YAML entry (docs/site_nav.yaml) and the quickstart content (docs/rag-plugin-sdk.md) with runnable code blocks and an interactive sandbox link to the local devcontainer.

Checklist (author):
- [x] Add SDK and adapters
- [x] Add docs + site nav entry
- [x] Devcontainer and local test harness
- [x] CI smoke workflow
- [x] Integration tests for adapters

Commits (summary):
- feat: initial plugin-sdk and types + runtime helpers
- feat: postgres-vector-adapter with integration tests
- feat: pdf-tool-adapter and fixtures
- docs: quickstart guide and site nav entry
- ci: add workflow quickstart-smoke.yml
- chore: add devcontainer and docker-compose services
- fix: minor type errors discovered during review

Files changed (high-level):
- examples/rag-plugin-sdk-quickstart/README.md
- examples/rag-plugin-sdk-quickstart/sdk/index.ts
- examples/rag-plugin-sdk-quickstart/sdk/types.ts
- examples/rag-plugin-sdk-quickstart/adapters/postgres-vector-adapter/*
- examples/rag-plugin-sdk-quickstart/adapters/pdf-tool-adapter/*
- examples/rag-plugin-sdk-quickstart/tests/*
- docs/site_nav.yaml
- docs/rag-plugin-sdk.md
- .devcontainer/Dockerfile
- .devcontainer/docker-compose.yml
- .github/workflows/quickstart-smoke.yml

Review thread (highlights):
- Ben Walker: "Nice ergonomics. Can we make the capability descriptor immutable and include a version field for future compatibility?" — Aisha: "Agree, added descriptor.version and froze the object in sdk/index.ts — see last commit." 
- Priya Natarajan: "Integration tests look good, but the sqlite vector test doesn't exercise similarity scoring thresholds. Please add a negative-case test." — Aisha: "Added tests/vector_similarity_negative.test.ts and updated adapter to expose a confidence field in results." 
- Luca Moreno: "Docs look solid. Suggest a short troubleshooting section for common devcontainer issues and a note on credential handling for private DBs." — Aisha: "Added troubleshooting and a note about KMS-backed envvar injection for Private deployments."

CI summary: GitHub Actions matrix ran on node 18 and node 20; checks: build (pass), lint (pass with warnings fixed), unit (pass), integration (pass), docs-linkcheck (pass). One intermittent test flake in earlier run (sqlite lock) was addressed by serializing the integration tests in the harness.

Testing plan: Manual verification steps added to docs/quickstart.md (how to run devcontainer, run smoke workflow locally, run adapters against fixtures). Integration tests included in CI; maintainers can run npm run test:integration to reproduce.

Compatibility and risk: The SDK is purely in examples and has no runtime dependency on the Redwood serving runtime. It follows the same types used in the public SDKs (redwood-sdk-typescript) but is intentionally isolated so it can be copied by partners. No code in production packages was changed.

Follow-ups: (tracked in ENG-4389)
- Add a Java/Python reference implementation of the Plugin SDK
- Publish a partner template and npm package for the SDK
- Add e2e scenario that exercises plugin fallback when a tool times out

Merge outcome: Squash-merged to main after approvals from all reviewers. Back-ported the quickstart documentation link to the v2026.02 docs snapshot channel.

title: Introduce RAG Seedkit: guided-playground CLI, flow templates, and tool-adapter spec
preview:
Motivation: onboarding teams repeatedly ask for a compact, opinionated starter that demonstrates: 1) a small guided CLI that walks users through building a RAG flow, 2) authorable flow templates (decision points + tool bindings), and 3) an explicit minimal tool-adapter spec (Go + TS examples) that shows how to register tools with the agent runtime. This PR introduces the 'seedkit' quickstart under examples/seedkit and follows the same testable patterns we use across other quickstarts. Checklist:
- [x] scaffolding for guided-playground CLI (TS) with step assertions
- [x] three flow templates (basic-qna, multi-tool, sessioned-chat)
- [x] minimal tool-adapter spec and Go shim example
- [x] structured-output validators and example evals
- [x] CI smoke tests and simple unit tests for the CLI runner
Description of changes (single-string summary): Adds a new examples/seedkit package with a small CLI that runs locally, a set of JSON flow templates that exercise retrieval+tools+streaming, a tool-adapter spec document (OPENAPI-like minimal schema) and two reference adapters (typescript shim + small Go binary). The CLI includes an interactive 'guided' mode that prompts users for choices and emits an execution trace compatible with our replay tooling. Commits: see commits field. Files changed (high level): see files_changed field. CI: initial runs failed on lint for TS imports; fixed in second commit by aligning eslint config with repo root. Observability: the quickstart emits a lightweight telemetry envelope (env-var enabled) that demonstrates the shape we recommend in docs for event-level tracing. Security: tool adapters run as separate processes by default in the examples to emphasise isolation; the README highlights KMS/HSM considerations for production. Usage: the README contains three short walkthroughs and a one-command local smoke run that seeds a tiny in-memory index and executes a template. Related: references ENG-8420 (seedkits roadmap) and DOC-1598 (doc updates to quickstart index).

title: Introduce async tool conductor playground with cooperative tool-runner and stepwise traces
preview:
Motivation: developers need a small, focused example showing how to orchestrate multiple asynchronous tool calls from a RAG-style agent while preserving stepwise debug traces and streaming partial tool outputs back to the client. This playground demonstrates a cooperative "tool conductor" pattern: tools register as long-running, cancellable actors that emit intermediate events (progress, partial results, diagnostics). The conductor coordinates these actors, merges partial outputs into the agent response stream, and records compact stepwise traces for postmortem/eval.

What this PR adds: a new lightweight example under examples/async-conductor that contains: a TypeScript playground server that simulates async tools (index.ts), a small agent shim that shows how to do incremental streaming and tool fallback, README usage and tutorial, telemetry probe presets for the Console, and e2e smoke CI to ensure the example boots and runs. Also adds a docs site page that explains the pattern and provides a guided walkthrough and evaluation checklist. The description below contains usage notes, design decisions, and a short how-to for consumers.

Usage highlights: run `yarn workspace examples async-conductor start` (script added) to boot the mini-playground. The conductor exposes an HTTP SSE endpoint `/v1/stream` that yields incremental deltas: assistant tokens, tool events (progress/partial_result), and final trace metadata. The README includes a minimal client snippet to rehydrate a streaming UI and attach the step trace.

Design notes: the conductor uses an internal event bus (in-memory for the playground) and an explicit per-request trace id. Tools implement a small async interface (start, cancel, status) and return partial items typed as `ToolEvent`. The agent layer treats tool events as prompt deltas and merges them using a simple policy (priority: tool partials > assistant continue tokens). The pattern is deliberately lightweight to be copy-paste friendly for teams building longer-running tool integrations (web scraping jobs, paginated DB exports, external search reopenings).

Testing and telemetry: added compact telemetry probes that surface: tool latency histogram, tool partial-rate (percentage of tools that emit at least one partial), conductor queue depth, and a sample request trace that shows stepwise timestamps for tool start/emit/finish. CI includes a smoke e2e that exercises a simulated long-running tool and asserts streaming semantics (partial events seen within 500ms, final trace fields present).

Compatibility: the example is written against the public examples SDKs (TS) and keeps dependencies minimal. No runtime changes required to Redwood core; this is an examples-only addition to illustrate patterns users can adopt on Hosted/Dedicated/Private.

Checklist performed: - [x] Example code and README - [x] Docs site page and tutorial - [x] Telemetry probe presets - [x] CI smoke test - [x] Reviewer feedback incorporated

Related: linked Linear tickets ENG-3124 (async tooling pattern), ENG-3198 (example telemetry probes).

title: Introduce RAG Playbook Simulator and Compact Authoring Kit
preview: Motivation: onboarding teams struggle to iterate on RAG playbooks without a lightweight local runner that mirrors Redwood's execution semantics. This PR introduces a compact Playbook Simulator (playbook-sim) and a small authoring kit to accelerate iterative authoring and smoke e2e testing. What it contains: 1) playbook-sim/runner: a node-based simulator that interprets playbook step graphs, executes stubbed tool calls, and supports snapshot/replay for deterministic e2e checks; 2) authoring/compact-ui: a minimal web UI (React) showing step sequencing, scenario presets, and telemetry probe toggles for quick validation; 3) docs/quickstarts/playbook-sim-quickstart.md: single-page quickstart integrated into the docs site with copy-paste examples and troubleshooting notes; 4) telemetry/probes: small SDK hooks for emitting compact probes used by the Console smoke harness; 5) tests/smoke: two deterministic replay fixtures used by CI. Migration/compat: simulator follows the playbook JSON v0.3 schema and includes a compatibility shim for older v0.2 snippets. Checklist: [x] add runner and replay snapshots; [x] add compact UI and example presets; [x] write quickstart and diagrams; [x] add CI smoke tests; [x] add Linear links and changelog note. Links: quickstart preview https://redwood.dev/docs/playbook-sim (staged), Linear ENG-7342. How to use: clone repo; cd redwood-examples/playbook-sim; yarn install; yarn dev to run UI and yarn smoke to run deterministic replay. Security: authoring UI runs locally only by default, telemetry is opt-in and documented. Follow-ups: add TypeScript SDK bindings for programmatic playbook generation (ENG-7428).

title: Introduce external-connector tool-calling samples + docs (retry/backoff & idempotency)
preview:
Summary: Adds a small, focused example set and docs demonstrating best practices for tool-calling external connectors (HTTP APIs, webhooks, and database adaptors) from RAG agents and tool-calling flows. The goal is to give engineers a reference for safe external I/O: retries with jittered backoff, idempotency tokens, short-circuiting on known transient errors, authentication patterns (token rotation + KMS/HSM hints), and a lightweight local emulator for offline testing.

Motivation and context: We've seen multiple customer support threads and internal runbooks where integrations with downstream APIs cause silent duplicates, unbounded retries, or leakage of credentials during local development (see ENG-2345). This PR collects a minimal, copy-pasteable sample set plus docs to reduce integration time and avoid common pitfalls. It also feeds a quickstart entry on the docs site so users can find the pattern when wiring tool-calling into their agent.

What this PR includes (high-level):
- End-to-end examples: TypeScript and Python connector stubs showing authenticated calls, exponential backoff with jitter, idempotency header support, and circuit-breaker heuristics.
- Quickstart: docs/quickstart that walks from "hello connector" to running an agent that calls a mock external service.
- Local emulator: a tiny bash+node emulator for CI and local dev to simulate transient failures, 429 throttles, and slow responses.
- Tests: a basic integration smoke workflow (GitHub Actions) and a local script to exercise retry scenarios.
- Docs: two new docs pages added to redwood-docs (reference + tutorial) and the example README linking to the quickstart and emulator.

Commit summary:
- 0f3a9c2: initial skeleton of connectors example and README
- a1b7d55: add TypeScript example and devcontainer for quickstart dev loop
- d4c120b: add Python example and idempotency helper; add docs pages
- e2b44f6: add emulator script and GitHub Actions smoke workflow; wire quickstart link in docs nav

Files changed (high-level):
- redwood-examples/connectors/*: new example library and README + devcontainer
- redwood-docs/reference/tool-calling/connectors.md: new reference describing connector API patterns and recommended headers
- redwood-docs/tutorials/tool-calling-reliability.md: tutorial with stepwise instructions and sample prompts
- .github/workflows/connectors-smoke.yml: lightweight CI to run emulator + sample script

Notes on design decisions:
- Examples are intentionally minimal and opinionated: using explicit idempotency tokens (Idempotency-Key header) rather than opaque dedupe inside the agent. This maps well to common third-party APIs and avoids coupling agent internals to downstream semantics.
- Backoff uses decorrelated jitter (referenced in tutorial) with a configurable max attempts guard inside the connector helper.
- Auth examples show short-lived tokens and a rotation-ready placeholder that references KMS but does not require a KMS integration to run the quickstart emulator.

Testing and CI:
- CI: the new connectors-smoke workflow spins up the emulator container and runs the TypeScript sample end-to-end, asserting no duplicate invocations after simulated retries. The CI currently runs on PR and merges only after green checks.
- Local testing: devcontainer and integration_emulator.sh provide a reproducible dev loop that simulates 429/500/timeout behaviour.

Review conversation (excerpt):
- Samir Patel (reviewer): "Nice focused example. A couple of asks: 1) can we make the default retry attempts lower in CI to avoid long runs? 2) include a short note about when NOT to retry (e.g. 4xx with application-level errors)."
- Priya Nair (author): "Reply: lowered CI attempts to 3 and added explicit logic to opt-out retries for 4xx except 429. Added a paragraph and code comment on rationale."
- Diego Martinez (reviewer): "Can you add a short snippet for webhook signature verification? I often see customers miss this in quickstarts."
- Priya Nair: "Added a webhook_signature.md snippet and referenced it from the reference doc. Kept signature examples generic (HMAC) to avoid tying to a specific provider."
- Ava Chen (reviewer, docs): "Docs look good; small nit on naming (connector vs adapter). Suggested renaming one header for clarity."
- Priya Nair: "Applied rename and updated cross-references."
- CI: initial run failed due to flaky emulator start timing; fixed by adding a readiness probe in emulator and re-run succeeded.

Merge strategy: squash merge. The branch was squashed into a single commit at merge time and merged to main after approvals (2 approvals).

Backwards compatibility and risk: This change is purely additive: examples and docs only. No runtime or API surface changes to the platform. Marked breaking_change: false.

Follow-ups (recommended):
- Add a compact checklist in the console docs when teams enable tool-calling in prod: auth posture, rate limit policies, cost accounting per-tool.
- Expand emulator to simulate per-tenant quotas for future Dedicated/Private testing (follow-up ENG ticket created).

How to try locally (quickstart excerpt):
1) git clone && cd redwood-examples/connectors
2) ./integration_emulator.sh start (spins up local mock)
3) in another shell: devcontainer or npm run start:ts or python examples/connector_examples.py --target http://localhost:8081
4) Observe logs; run integration_emulator.sh simulate-throttle to test 429 backoff

If you want to review the implementation details, the docs page includes code excerpts and a suggested audit log pattern for connector invocations to help SRE and security teams reason about retries and duplicate suppression.

TL;DR: small, practical example set + docs that reduce the time-to-safe-integration for tool-calling external APIs.
text:
Summary: Adds a small, focused example set and docs demonstrating best practices for tool-calling external connectors (HTTP APIs, webhooks, and database adaptors) from RAG agents and tool-calling flows. The goal is to give engineers a reference for safe external I/O: retries with jittered backoff, idempotency tokens, short-circuiting on known transient errors, authentication patterns (token rotation + KMS/HSM hints), and a lightweight local emulator for offline testing.

Motivation and context: We've seen multiple customer support threads and internal runbooks where integrations with downstream APIs cause silent duplicates, unbounded retries, or leakage of credentials during local development (see ENG-2345). This PR collects a minimal, copy-pasteable sample set plus docs to reduce integration time and avoid common pitfalls. It also feeds a quickstart entry on the docs site so users can find the pattern when wiring tool-calling into their agent.

What this PR includes (high-level):
- End-to-end examples: TypeScript and Python connector stubs showing authenticated calls, exponential backoff with jitter, idempotency header support, and circuit-breaker heuristics.
- Quickstart: docs/quickstart that walks from "hello connector" to running an agent that calls a mock external service.
- Local emulator: a tiny bash+node emulator for CI and local dev to simulate transient failures, 429 throttles, and slow responses.
- Tests: a basic integration smoke workflow (GitHub Actions) and a local script to exercise retry scenarios.
- Docs: two new docs pages added to redwood-docs (reference + tutorial) and the example README linking to the quickstart and emulator.

Commit summary:
- 0f3a9c2: initial skeleton of connectors example and README
- a1b7d55: add TypeScript example and devcontainer for quickstart dev loop
- d4c120b: add Python example and idempotency helper; add docs pages
- e2b44f6: add emulator script and GitHub Actions smoke workflow; wire quickstart link in docs nav

Files changed (high-level):
- redwood-examples/connectors/*: new example library and README + devcontainer
- redwood-docs/reference/tool-calling/connectors.md: new reference describing connector API patterns and recommended headers
- redwood-docs/tutorials/tool-calling-reliability.md: tutorial with stepwise instructions and sample prompts
- .github/workflows/connectors-smoke.yml: lightweight CI to run emulator + sample script

Notes on design decisions:
- Examples are intentionally minimal and opinionated: using explicit idempotency tokens (Idempotency-Key header) rather than opaque dedupe inside the agent. This maps well to common third-party APIs and avoids coupling agent internals to downstream semantics.
- Backoff uses decorrelated jitter (referenced in tutorial) with a configurable max attempts guard inside the connector helper.
- Auth examples show short-lived tokens and a rotation-ready placeholder that references KMS but does not require a KMS integration to run the quickstart emulator.

Testing and CI:
- CI: the new connectors-smoke workflow spins up the emulator container and runs the TypeScript sample end-to-end, asserting no duplicate invocations after simulated retries. The CI currently runs on PR and merges only after green checks.
- Local testing: devcontainer and integration_emulator.sh provide a reproducible dev loop that simulates 429/500/timeout behaviour.

Review conversation (excerpt):
- Samir Patel (reviewer): "Nice focused example. A couple of asks: 1) can we make the default retry attempts lower in CI to avoid long runs? 2) include a short note about when NOT to retry (e.g. 4xx with application-level errors)."
- Priya Nair (author): "Reply: lowered CI attempts to 3 and added explicit logic to opt-out retries for 4xx except 429. Added a paragraph and code comment on rationale."
- Diego Martinez (reviewer): "Can you add a short snippet for webhook signature verification? I often see customers miss this in quickstarts."
- Priya Nair: "Added a webhook_signature.md snippet and referenced it from the reference doc. Kept signature examples generic (HMAC) to avoid tying to a specific provider."
- Ava Chen (reviewer, docs): "Docs look good; small nit on naming (connector vs adapter). Suggested renaming one header for clarity."
- Priya Nair: "Applied rename and updated cross-references."
- CI: initial run failed due to flaky emulator start timing; fixed by adding a readiness probe in emulator and re-run succeeded.

Merge strategy: squash merge. The branch was squashed into a single commit at merge time and merged to main after approvals (2 approvals).

Backwards compatibility and risk: This change is purely additive: examples and docs only. No runtime or API surface changes to the platform. Marked breaking_change: false.

Follow-ups (recommended):
- Add a compact checklist in the console docs when teams enable tool-calling in prod: auth posture, rate limit policies, cost accounting per-tool.
- Expand emulator to simulate per-tenant quotas for future Dedicated/Private testing (follow-up ENG ticket created).

How to try locally (quickstart excerpt):
1) git clone && cd redwood-examples/connectors
2) ./integration_emulator.sh start (spins up local mock)
3) in another shell: devcontainer or npm run start:ts or python examples/connector_examples.py --target http://localhost:8081
4) Observe logs; run integration_emulator.sh simulate-throttle to test 429 backoff

If you want to review the implementation details, the docs page includes code excerpts and a suggested audit log pattern for connector invocations to help SRE and security teams reason about retries and duplicate suppression.

TL;DR: small, practical example set + docs that reduce the time-to-safe-integration for tool-calling external APIs.

title: Add minimal chat app template to accelerate Hosted API activation
preview:
This PR adds a small, copy-pasteable "minimal chat app" example intended to reduce time-to-first-token for new Hosted API users. The goal is to provide a single folder that can be run in <5 minutes with an API key, supports streaming by default, and matches the onboarding email + console nudge CTAs ("Make your first request" / "Enable streaming"). It also includes lightweight telemetry guidance so the Growth funnel can attribute activation accelerators without collecting sensitive payloads.

What’s included: (1) New example at examples/minimal-chat-app/ implemented as a Node + Express server with a tiny static UI. Server proxies to Redwood Hosted API using the Redwood TS SDK, streams tokens to the browser via SSE, and shows basic error states (missing/invalid API key, rate limited). (2) README updates: top-level README now highlights the minimal chat app as the recommended first project for Hosted API onboarding; the example README includes a “First request” section, troubleshooting, and a short note on best practices (do not expose API keys in the browser, rotate keys, prefer server-side calls). (3) Telemetry guidance: adds a section describing optional instrumentation events aligned with the activation funnel taxonomy (e.g., example_app_started, example_first_request_sent, example_streaming_enabled, example_error_seen) and explicitly states that prompts/completions must not be logged. Telemetry is opt-in via an env var and defaults to off in the template.

Why: During the lifecycle growth experiments sprint, we observed that many new signups stall between API key creation and first successful request. A minimal runnable template is a high-leverage way to reduce friction, and it creates a stable target for onboarding emails and console nudges to link to.

Notes/guardrails: The template intentionally avoids any client-side Redwood calls to prevent key leakage. The telemetry examples only include non-content metadata (timestamps, booleans, coarse error categories) and are documented as optional. No changes to production code; examples repo only.

Rollout: Once merged, Marketing/DevEx will update onboarding email CTAs to deep-link into the minimal chat app README and pin it in the console onboarding checklist. Follow-up task (separate) will add a Docker option if we see install issues on Windows.

title: Add production-ready streaming chat example (retries/backoff, structured logs, health check)
preview:
Context: We’ve seen repeated onboarding + early-prod issues around streaming disconnects and “we hit 429 immediately” surprises (SUP-18488 / SUP-18510). As part of the onboarding checklist work, we want a reference example that is explicitly production-oriented (not just a minimal demo).

This PR adds a Node.js + TypeScript streaming chat example that shows: (1) server-side proxying of streaming responses (so API keys are not shipped to the browser), (2) safe retry/backoff patterns for rate limits and transient gateway errors, (3) structured JSON logging that is usable in production, and (4) a simple health check endpoint for container orchestration.

What’s included (high-level): - New example: examples/streaming-chat-prod-ready - Express server with endpoints: GET /healthz (liveness/readiness), POST /v1/chat/stream (SSE streaming proxy) - Redwood API call uses streaming mode and forwards tokens as SSE events - Retry strategy: exponential backoff + full jitter for 429, 408, 409, 425, 500, 502, 503, 504 and select network errors - Per-request timeout + abort handling; demonstrates client disconnect cleanup - Structured logging via pino: request_id, route, model, status_code, latency_ms, attempt, retry_reason - Log redaction: prompts/messages are NOT logged by default; optional DEBUG_LOG_PROMPTS for local debugging only - Minimal “smoke” script + README with deploy notes and common failure modes (429 handling, disconnects)

Why this approach: - We proxy streaming via server to keep secrets off the client and to centralize retries/timeouts. - Retries are constrained to idempotent-ish failure modes and require explicit opt-in for retrying after bytes have started streaming (we do not do that by default). - Logging is JSON so it can be shipped to whatever logging backend customers use.

Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Add OpenTelemetry tracing example (kept out of scope for MVP to avoid bringing in heavier deps). - Add a small “budget/quota guardrail” snippet to the README once Console + docs links settle.

Testing: - yarn test (unit tests for retry/backoff and SSE framing) - yarn lint - manual: ran locally and simulated 429/503 via test middleware; validated disconnect cleanup and that we don’t retry after streaming starts.

Screenshots/notes: N/A (example repo).

title: Route experiments sample app: sticky key support + variant header debugging
preview:
Summary: Adds an updated route experiments sample app demonstrating (1) passing a stable stickiness key to ensure deterministic variant assignment and (2) reading Redwood experiment/variant response headers to debug routing in the field.

Context: With route-level experiments rolling into Console Rollouts, multiple beta customers asked for a concrete end-to-end example showing what to send from clients and what to log when assignment looks skewed. Support also reported confusion about which headers to inspect during triage (see SUP-24831).

What’s included (high level):
- Adds an explicit `stickyKey` input to the sample UI and server routes.
- Sends stickiness key using the request header `x-redwood-sticky-key` (preferred) with a safe fallback to a stable application-level identifier when available.
- Logs and displays the experiment assignment response headers:
  - `x-redwood-experiment-id`
  - `x-redwood-variant-id`
  - `x-redwood-config-version` (if present)
  - `x-request-id` (for correlating with traces)
- Adds a small “Debug assignment” panel that prints the above headers plus the resolved route ID and model ID used.
- Updates README with recommended stickiness key strategies, gotchas, and troubleshooting steps.

Why this approach:
- We want the stickiness key to be stable across sessions (e.g., customer_id / user_id) and high entropy to avoid skew when identifiers are missing.
- The sample intentionally does NOT default to IP address or User-Agent hashing since those are brittle and can break determinism across regions/proxies.

Implementation notes:
- Node/Express example: wraps Redwood client calls and ensures the stickiness header is forwarded for both streaming and non-streaming requests.
- Browser example: uses an input field for stickiness key, but the README calls out that production apps should use a stable account identifier.
- Header parsing is case-insensitive; the example normalizes to lower-case for display.
- For streaming responses, we show the headers immediately after the HTTP response is established (before consuming the stream) so users can debug even if the stream errors.

How to test:
1) Enable route experiments for your account (beta flag required in some regions).
2) Create an experiment on a route used by this sample app (Console → Rollouts → Experiments).
3) Run the example:
   - `pnpm install`
   - `pnpm dev`
4) Set the same stickiness key and refresh multiple times → assignment should remain stable (variant id does not change).
5) Change stickiness key → you should observe different assignment depending on the experiment split.

Troubleshooting guide added:
- If you see variant skew: verify you are actually sending `x-redwood-sticky-key` and that it’s not empty/low entropy.
- If the assignment looks inconsistent: verify proxies/load balancers are not stripping custom headers.
- If Console results don’t update: cross-check `x-redwood-config-version` and experiment start time; results may lag due to aggregation windows.

Security/privacy:
- README includes explicit guidance to avoid using raw emails or other sensitive identifiers as the stickiness key. Prefer a stable opaque ID.

Checklist:
- [x] Local run + manual verification of deterministic assignment
- [x] Streaming route test (headers visible even when stream consumes)
- [x] README updated with header names and recommendations
- [x] Lint/format

Follow-ups (not in this PR):
- Align header names with final docs wording once `redwood-docs#1447` lands.
- Add a minimal Python CLI example for support/oncall use (separate PR).

title: Add agent tooling example with safe defaults (guardrails + structured logs)
preview: Context: As part of the RAG + agents quickstart workstream, we want examples that demonstrate “production-minded by default” patterns (safe defaults, guardrails, and operational hooks) without forcing users into a full template scaffold. This PR adds a new agent tooling example that shows (1) a bounded agent loop (max iterations + max wall time), (2) strict tool schema + tool output size limits, (3) resilient request settings (timeouts + retry/backoff for non-streaming calls; streaming is out-of-scope here), and (4) structured logs with correlation IDs and redaction. What’s included: - New example at examples/agents/tooling-safe-defaults/ with a small HTTP server exposing POST /agent/run and a couple of demo tools (search_docs + get_account_status). - Agent loop with guardrails: max_iterations (default 6), max_tool_calls (default 10), max_tool_output_bytes (default 32KB), per-request deadline (default 25s), and tool whitelist enforcement. - Tool validation: request->tool args validated against JSON schema (zod in TS); tool output is truncated and annotated if it exceeds limits; tool errors are wrapped in a typed error with user-safe messaging. - Logging: JSON logs (pino) with fields: level, ts, msg, redwood.request_id (if present), trace_id/span_id (if OTel is configured), agent.iteration, tool.name, tool.status, error.type. Logs redact API keys and obvious secrets by default. - README: setup, env var contract, “why these defaults”, and a troubleshooting section (401/429/timeouts/tool schema failures). - Minimal smoke test (node:test) that exercises: a) invalid tool args -> tool validation error path, b) tool exception -> user-safe error surfaced, c) max_iterations reached -> agent aborts with explicit reason. Config: Uses env vars consistent with the template conventions: REDWOOD_API_KEY (required), REDWOOD_MODEL (default: redwood/verified-small), REDWOOD_BASE_URL (optional), AGENT_MAX_ITERATIONS, AGENT_MAX_TOOL_CALLS, AGENT_MAX_TOOL_OUTPUT_BYTES, AGENT_DEADLINE_MS. Notes / non-goals: - This is an example (not the template). It intentionally avoids a full OTel collector setup; however, it propagates request IDs into logs and includes optional OTel hooks if users already have an SDK/collector. - No vector store / retrieval pipeline here; the focus is tool calling + safe loop controls. Illustrative snippet (agent loop guardrails): const maxIterations = cfg.maxIterations ?? 6; const deadlineAt = Date.now() + (cfg.deadlineMs ?? 25_000); for (let i = 0; i < maxIterations; i++) { if (Date.now() > deadlineAt) throw new AgentAbortError('deadline_exceeded'); ... if (toolCalls > maxToolCalls) throw new AgentAbortError('max_tool_calls_exceeded'); } throw new AgentAbortError('max_iterations_exceeded'); Follow-ups: - Once the template’s error taxonomy stabilizes, we should align error.type values in this example to match the template taxonomy exactly. - Consider adding a second example showing streaming + reconnect once the TS SDK streaming improvements land (tracked in ENG-4186).
